content:
# Foreword

*By Sunny R Gupta*

---

I didn't study computer science.

I didn't crack JEE. I didn't get the branch I wanted. I graduated as something adjacent to software engineering — close enough to borrow the vocabulary, far enough to always feel like I was sneaking into the party.

And yet, somehow, I spent the last decade building software for millions of people. At startups, at Atlassian, and now at JioHotstar — one of India's largest streaming platforms. I've led engineering teams, shipped products at scale, and watched the entire industry I work in get turned sideways by a technology that nobody fully saw coming.

That technology, of course, is AI.

---

I started the "for Bunnies" series because I was tired of technical writing that assumed you were already an expert — or that treated you like one if you weren't. The first piece, a system design explainer published in January 2026, got fifty reposts and a comment that said: *"This is the first time system design has made sense to me."*

That comment stuck. Because it told me something I already suspected: the problem was never the reader's intelligence. It was always the writing.

Most technical content is written to impress peers, not to teach beginners. It leads with jargon. It skips the "why." It assumes the context that beginners are still building. And when beginners bounce off it — which they do, constantly — we somehow blame the beginners.

I don't believe in that. I never have.

---

This book is the one I wish had existed when I was trying to figure out how to make AI do something *useful*.

Not the theory. I've read the theory. I've nodded along to papers I half-understood and benchmark comparisons I'd already forgotten by the next morning. What I needed — what most people building things actually need — is the practical stuff. How do I actually call this API? What do I put in the system prompt? Why is my app hallucinating? How do I deploy this without accidentally exposing my API key?

Those are the questions this book answers.

---

Here's something I've noticed in fifteen-plus years of building software: the people who ship things aren't always the most technically sophisticated. They're the ones who got comfortable enough with the fundamentals to start, stubborn enough to push through the confusing parts, and honest enough to ask basic questions without embarrassment.

AI has made that group dramatically larger. The gap between "person with an idea" and "person who shipped the idea" has never been smaller. But you still have to close it yourself. Nobody ships anything by reading.

So: read this book with your laptop open. Actually do the exercises. When you break something — and you will break things — treat it as information, not failure. The app that breaks teaches you more than the app that works on the first try.

I wrote this for people who are at the beginning. Who feel like they're not supposed to be here. Who have an idea but don't know where to start.

You're exactly who I wrote it for. The beginning is exactly where you're supposed to be.

Let's build something that matters.

---

**Sunny R Gupta**  
Engineering Leader, JioHotstar  
Founder, TeamShiksha & LithSocial  
Bengaluru, India  
May 2026

*Find me at [linkedin.com/in/sunnykgupta](https://linkedin.com/in/sunnykgupta) or [@sunnykgupta](https://x.com/sunnykgupta) on X.*
